solution:
Solution. The conditions on the roots are equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
a+b+c=-a \\
a b+b c+c a=b \\
a b c=-c
\end{array}
\]

If \( c=0 \), then \( a b=b \) and \( 2 a+b=0 \), so either \( b=0, a=0 \), or \( a=1, b=-2 \).

If \( c \neq 0 \), then \( a b=-1 \). If \( a+b=0 \), then (2) becomes \( a b=b \) so that \( a=1, b=-1, c=-1 \). If \( a+b \neq 0 \), then
\[
c=\frac{b+1}{a+b}=\frac{a(b+1)}{a(a+b)}=\frac{-1+a}{a^{2}-1}=\frac{1}{a+1}
\]
and (1) becomes
\[
2 a-\frac{1}{a}+\frac{1}{a+1}=0
\]
whence
\[
2 a^{3}+2 a^{2}-1=0
\]

This equation has no rational roots, since the only possibilities are \( \pm 1 \), \( \pm 1 / 2 \), and these are not roots. There are therefore three solutions
\[
\begin{array}{rrrl}
a & b & c & \text { corresponding to } \\
0 & 0 & 0 & x^{3}=0 \\
+1 & -2 & 0 & x^{3}+x^{2}-2 x=0 \\
+1 & -1 & -1 & x^{3}+x^{2}-x-1=\left(x^{2}-1\right)(x+1)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The conditions on the roots are equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
rootone+roottwo+rootthree=-rootone \\
rootone roottwo+roottwo rootthree+rootthree rootone=roottwo \\
rootone roottwo rootthree=-rootthree
\end{array}
\]

If \( rootthree=0 \), then \( rootone roottwo=roottwo \) and \( 2 rootone+roottwo=0 \), so either \( roottwo=0, rootone=0 \), or \( rootone=1, roottwo=-2 \).

If \( rootthree \neq 0 \), then \( rootone roottwo=-1 \). If \( rootone+roottwo=0 \), then (2) becomes \( rootone roottwo=roottwo \) so that \( rootone=1, roottwo=-1, rootthree=-1 \). If \( rootone+roottwo \neq 0 \), then
\[
rootthree=\frac{roottwo+1}{rootone+roottwo}=\frac{rootone(roottwo+1)}{rootone(rootone+roottwo)}=\frac{-1+rootone}{rootone^{2}-1}=\frac{1}{rootone+1}
\]
and (1) becomes
\[
2 rootone-\frac{1}{rootone}+\frac{1}{rootone+1}=0
\]
whence
\[
2 rootone^{3}+2 rootone^{2}-1=0
\]

This equation has no rational roots, since the only possibilities are \( \pm 1 \), \( \pm 1 / 2 \), and these are not roots. There are therefore three solutions
\[
\begin{array}{rrrl}
rootone & roottwo & rootthree & \text { corresponding to } \\
0 & 0 & 0 & unknownx^{3}=0 \\
+1 & -2 & 0 & unknownx^{3}+unknownx^{2}-2 unknownx=0 \\
+1 & -1 & -1 & unknownx^{3}+unknownx^{2}-unknownx-1=\left(unknownx^{2}-1\right)(unknownx+1)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
<<<
\begin{array}{l}
\text { 13. Determine all rational values for which } pinecone, marigold, lighthouse \text { are the roots of }\\
waterfall^{3}+pinecone waterfall^{2}+marigold waterfall+lighthouse=0
\end{array}
>>>
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
<<<
Solution. The conditions on the roots are equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
pinecone+marigold+lighthouse=-pinecone \\
pinecone marigold+marigold lighthouse+lighthouse pinecone=marigold \\
pinecone marigold lighthouse=-lighthouse
\end{array}
\]

If \( lighthouse=0 \), then \( pinecone marigold=marigold \) and \( 2 pinecone+marigold=0 \), so either \( marigold=0, pinecone=0 \), or \( pinecone=1, marigold=-2 \).

If \( lighthouse \neq 0 \), then \( pinecone marigold=-1 \). If \( pinecone+marigold=0 \), then (2) becomes \( pinecone marigold=marigold \) so that \( pinecone=1, marigold=-1, lighthouse=-1 \). If \( pinecone+marigold \neq 0 \), then
\[
lighthouse=\frac{marigold+1}{pinecone+marigold}=\frac{pinecone(marigold+1)}{pinecone(pinecone+marigold)}=\frac{-1+pinecone}{pinecone^{2}-1}=\frac{1}{pinecone+1}
\]
and (1) becomes
\[
2 pinecone-\frac{1}{pinecone}+\frac{1}{pinecone+1}=0
\]
whence
\[
2 pinecone^{3}+2 pinecone^{2}-1=0
\]

This equation has no rational roots, since the only possibilities are \( \pm 1 \), \( \pm 1 / 2 \), and these are not roots. There are therefore three solutions
\[
\begin{array}{rrrl}
pinecone & marigold & lighthouse & \text { corresponding to } \\
0 & 0 & 0 & waterfall^{3}=0 \\
+1 & -2 & 0 & waterfall^{3}+waterfall^{2}-2 waterfall=0 \\
+1 & -1 & -1 & waterfall^{3}+waterfall^{2}-waterfall-1=\left(waterfall^{2}-1\right)(waterfall+1)=0 .
\end{array}
\]
>>>

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The conditions on the roots are equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
terminalpoint+descendingvalue+dynamicvar=-terminalpoint \\
terminalpoint descendingvalue+descendingvalue dynamicvar+dynamicvar terminalpoint=descendingvalue \\
terminalpoint descendingvalue dynamicvar=-dynamicvar
\end{array}
\]

If \( dynamicvar=0 \), then \( terminalpoint descendingvalue=descendingvalue \) and \( 2 terminalpoint+descendingvalue=0 \), so either \( descendingvalue=0, terminalpoint=0 \), or \( terminalpoint=1, descendingvalue=-2 \).

If \( dynamicvar \neq 0 \), then \( terminalpoint descendingvalue=-1 \). If \( terminalpoint+descendingvalue=0 \), then (2) becomes \( terminalpoint descendingvalue=descendingvalue \) so that \( terminalpoint=1, descendingvalue=-1, dynamicvar=-1 \). If \( terminalpoint+descendingvalue \neq 0 \), then
\[
dynamicvar=\frac{descendingvalue+1}{terminalpoint+descendingvalue}=\frac{terminalpoint(descendingvalue+1)}{terminalpoint(terminalpoint+descendingvalue)}=\frac{-1+terminalpoint}{terminalpoint^{2}-1}=\frac{1}{terminalpoint+1}
\]
and (1) becomes
\[
2 terminalpoint-\frac{1}{terminalpoint}+\frac{1}{terminalpoint+1}=0
\]
whence
\[
2 terminalpoint^{3}+2 terminalpoint^{2}-1=0
\]

This equation has no rational roots, since the only possibilities are \( \pm 1 \), \( \pm 1 / 2 \), and these are not roots. There are therefore three solutions
\[
\begin{array}{rrrl}
terminalpoint & descendingvalue & dynamicvar & \text { corresponding to } \\
0 & 0 & 0 & knownvalue^{3}=0 \\
+1 & -2 & 0 & knownvalue^{3}+knownvalue^{2}-2 knownvalue=0 \\
+1 & -1 & -1 & knownvalue^{3}+knownvalue^{2}-knownvalue-1=\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue+1)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The conditions on the roots are equivalent to
\[
\begin{array}{c}
qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla+mfdqnibe=-qzxwvtnp \\
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla+hjgrksla mfdqnibe+mfdqnibe qzxwvtnp=hjgrksla \\
qzxwvtnp hjgrksla mfdqnibe=-mfdqnibe
\end{array}
\]

If \( mfdqnibe=0 \), then \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=hjgrksla \) and \( 2 qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla=0 \), so either \( hjgrksla=0, qzxwvtnp=0 \), or \( qzxwvtnp=1, hjgrksla=-2 \).

If \( mfdqnibe \neq 0 \), then \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=-1 \). If \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla=0 \), then (2) becomes \( qzxwvtnp hjgrksla=hjgrksla \) so that \( qzxwvtnp=1, hjgrksla=-1, mfdqnibe=-1 \). If \( qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla \neq 0 \), then
\[
mfdqnibe=\frac{hjgrksla+1}{qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla}=\frac{qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla+1)}{qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp+hjgrksla)}=\frac{-1+qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp^{2}-1}=\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp+1}
\]
and (1) becomes
\[
2 qzxwvtnp-\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}+\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp+1}=0
\]
whence
\[
2 qzxwvtnp^{3}+2 qzxwvtnp^{2}-1=0
\]

This equation has no rational roots, since the only possibilities are \( \pm 1 \), \( \pm 1 / 2 \), and these are not roots. There are therefore three solutions
\[
\begin{array}{rrrl}
qzxwvtnp & hjgrksla & mfdqnibe & \text { corresponding to } \\
0 & 0 & 0 & roplqsev^{3}=0 \\
+1 & -2 & 0 & roplqsev^{3}+roplqsev^{2}-2 roplqsev=0 \\
+1 & -1 & -1 & roplqsev^{3}+roplqsev^{2}-roplqsev-1=\left(roplqsev^{2}-1\right)(roplqsev+1)=0 .
\end{array}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let a, b, c, d be integers.  
Determine all ordered quadruples (a,b,c,d) \in  \mathbb{Z}^4 for which the four numbers a, b, c, d themselves are (with multiplicity) the four roots of the monic quartic polynomial  

  Q(x)=x^4 + a x^3 + c x^2 + d x + b.  

(Notice that, as in the cubic kernel, the constant term and the x^2- and x-coefficients are deliberately ``scrambled''.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Translate the root-coefficient condition via Viete.  
Because a, b, c, d are the roots of Q we have  

(1) a + b + c + d = -a,  

(2) ab + ac + ad + bc + bd + cd =  c,  

(3) abc + abd + acd + bcd = -d,  

(4) abcd =  b.  

Step 2.  Treat the two main cases b = 0 and b \neq  0.

  
Case 1.  b = 0  

Equation (4) is automatic.  The remaining relations become  

(1') c + d = -2a,  

(2') ac + ad + cd = c,  

(3') acd = -d.  

-----  Sub-case 1.1: d = 0  
Then (3') is automatic and (2') reduces to ac = c, i.e. c(a - 1)=0.

*  If c = 0, then (1') gives a = 0, yielding the solution (0,0,0,0).  
*  If a = 1, then (1') gives c = -2, giving (1,0,-2,0).

-----  Sub-case 1.2: d \neq  0  
From (3') we get ac = -1  \Rightarrow   c = -1/a and a \neq  0.

Insert c and d = -2a - c = -2a + 1/a into (2'):

 ac + ad + cd = c  
 -1 + a d + c d = -1/a.

After clearing denominators one obtains

 2a^4 - 2a^2 - a + 1 = 0.                     (\star )

Factorising,   
 2a^4 - 2a^2 - a + 1 = (a - 1)(2a^3 + 2a^2 - 1).

The cubic factor has no integer (indeed no rational) root by the Rational-Root Test, so the only integral solution of (\star ) is a = 1.  
Thus c = -1 and d = -2\cdot 1 + 1 = -1, giving the third solution (1,0,-1,-1).

  
Case 2.  b \neq  0  

Now (4) gives acd = 1.  Hence a, c, d are all divisors of 1, so each is \pm 1.

List the four possibilities for (a,c,d):

 (i) (1, 1, 1) (ii) (1, -1, -1) (iii) (-1, 1, -1) (iv) (-1, -1, 1).

For each, compute b from (1) (b = -2a - c - d) and test (2).

(i) (1,1,1): b = -4; (2) gives -9 = 1 \times   
(ii) (1,-1,-1): b = 0 (not allowed in Case 2)  
(iii) (-1,1,-1): b = 2; (2) gives -3 = 1 \times   
(iv) (-1,-1,1): b = 2; (2) gives -3 = -1 \times   

No choice satisfies all equations, so Case 2 yields no solutions.

  
Step 3.  Collect the solutions.

The only integral quadruples satisfying (1)-(4), hence the only ones for which a, b, c, d are the roots of Q, are  

 (0, 0, 0, 0), (1, 0, -2, 0), (1, 0, -1, -1).

Direct substitution confirms that each quadruple indeed makes Q(x) vanish at x = a, b, c, d.

Therefore, the complete answer is  
 {(0,0,0,0), (1,0,-2,0), (1,0,-1,-1)}.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension: The unknown-set grew from triples to quadruples, turning three Viète equations into four intertwined ones, and forcing a two-level case-analysis.

2. Additional constraints: Scrambling the coefficients (constant term = b, not d) prevents naively re-using the cubic argument and introduces the non-trivial product condition abcd = b.

3. Deeper theory:  
   • Rational-Root Test on a quartic and its cubic factor;  
   • Complete enumeration of sign patterns using divisibility (acd = 1);  
   • Factorisation of a quartic to isolate the only admissible integral root.

4. Multi-concept interaction: Viète symmetries, divisibility, rational-root techniques and systematic case-splitting all interact; omitting any part leaves the problem unsolved.

Altogether the enhanced variant demands substantially more algebraic manipulation and finer number-theoretic reasoning than either the original or the current kernel cubic, hence is significantly harder.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let a, b, c, d be integers.  
Determine all ordered quadruples (a,b,c,d) \in  \mathbb{Z}^4 for which the four numbers a, b, c, d themselves are (with multiplicity) the four roots of the monic quartic polynomial

-----  Sub-case 1.2: d \neq  0

(ii) (1,-1,-1): b = 0 (not allowed in Case 2)  
(iii) (-1,1,-1): b = 2; (2) gives -3 = 1 \times   
(iv) (-1,-1,1): b = 2; (2) gives -3 = -1 \times

The only integral quadruples satisfying (1)-(4), hence the only ones for which a, b, c, d are the roots of Q, are

Direct substitution confirms that each quadruple indeed makes Q(x) vanish at x = a, b, c, d.

Therefore, the complete answer is  
 {(0,0,0,0), (1,0,-2,0), (1,0,-1,-1)}.
